text: The surgeon suspends the uterus to improve visibility of the pelvis, preparing for a robotic sigmoid resection where the sigmoid colon is adhered to the left pelvis.
The surgeon examines adhesions in the pelvic region, noting the presence of a long, floppy mesentery while planning to preserve the superior hemorrhoidal artery.
The surgeon begins a lateral to medial dissection of the sigmoid colon, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the tissue layers.

The surgeon observes the redundant bowel with a long floppy mesentery, noting the sigmoid colon adhered to the left pelvic sidewall. The iliac vessels and left adnexa are visible as the surgeon lifts the sigmoid mesentery off the retroperitoneum, identifying the left ureter.
The surgeon demonstrates proximal mobilization of the bowel, highlighting the redundant and long floppy mesentery. The splenic flexure remains unmobilized due to the bowel's redundancy.

The surgeon attempts to elevate the sigmoid colon, which is adhered to the left adnexa, from the pelvis. The thickened rectal mesentery is visible, indicating the sigmoid colon's adherence and its position over the top of the pelvis.
The surgeon frees the distal epiploic appendages of the sigmoid colon from the rectal mesentery, aiding in the elevation of the sigmoid out of the pelvis.
The surgeon frees the distal sigmoid colon to visualize the planned transection margin on the rectum. The mesentery is then taken, revealing its long and floppy nature.
The surgeon carefully preserves the superior hemorrhoidal artery while hugging the bowel wall, preparing to take the mesentery up to the proximal margin.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to take the mesentery to the distal margin, encountering extremely thickened mesentery tissue that requires multiple firings to transect.

The surgeon works towards the upper rectum, cleaning the area around the distal target. The rectal region appears inflamed due to the sigmoid colon resting on top, but the surgeon prepares for transrectal extraction of the specimen.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) to check the margins, marking them for visibility under white light. The distal margin is then assessed before proceeding with the transection.

Again, these cases are done with four eight millimeter ports. I'm rarely using an assist these days, particularly for the more routine cases. And the transrectal extraction I think really has value in terms of the decrease in making pain from the extraction incision site in any SSI.

If you were not comfortable doing sewn anastomosis, which has become my preference in avoiding staplers, you can still go ahead and do transrectal extraction of your specimen and then place your anvil intercorporeally by placing it up through the open end of the rectum and then either purse string with a suture or an endo loop around the proximal and distal margins to bring the stapler together.

I'd like to morselate up the specimen to help get it out. In this case, and I edited out of the video, I actually desperately tried to place the wound protector the way that Eric Haas has taught us. I just really could not get the thing to open up into the rectum. And I ended up cutting the plastic on it so the rings kind of separated. So I just fell back and did my... my standard, which is just chopping it up and pulling it out. I won't give up on the wound protector because I think I could probably save about twenty minutes of not having to cut the piece of colon into tiny little pieces for extraction, considering I have to go below each time to pull out the specimen and then go sit down at the console.

But this technique does work well for me and it's something that I've done numerous times. There's always a concern I think from watching these videos of pelvic abscess given some of the spillage of some stool. I just wash everything out and I can just tell you that in a lot of these cases I've never had a post-op pelvic abscess.

The surgeon performs a sewn single-layer anastomosis, beginning posteriorly in the middle and sewing out to the far side on the left. The bowel is held without a stay suture, using two, three or six inch Velox sutures to achieve full thickness bites.

The surgeon performs an anastomosis with a well-matched proximal and distal bowel, taking even full-thickness bites. The anterior wall is imbricated, and the surgeon parks the needle before introducing a second one.

The surgeon continues the anastomosis by taking full thickness bites around the bowel, ensuring the sutures extend beyond the cut serosa. After assessing a previous bite as thin, the surgeon revisits the area to take a larger bite before proceeding with the suturing.

Having been a two layer anastomosis person most of my career, switching to single layer was a little bit scary for me. I think that in any location, if you have concerns about gaps or the way that it looks, placing another interrupted suture is always a good idea. the repetition of doing it and seeing what the bowel looks like. And then knowing the patient outcome helps decrease anxiety for the subsequent time you do one of these cases.
The surgeon cannels over the top of the anastomosis, ensuring the sutures end in the middle rather than at the edge, before returning to the other suture.

Again, this does take longer than if you were to place your anvil and staple, but you save the time by having done the transrectal extraction of the specimen. And if you were an extracorporeal anvil placer with a robotic case, you would have to undock the robot, make your incision, place your anvil, and then either redock or finish laparoscopically. So I think it's kind of time neutral. These cases take me... between two and a half and three hours, which I think is pretty reasonable.

Adding tap locks to essentially every single case that I do has been instrumental in further decreasing pain for my patients. And although they might have a standing PRN dose of narcotics while they're in the hospital, The vast majority of my patients will go home without any narcotics at all, which I think is a really good thing.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and performs a leak test using a rigid scope through the anastomosis. The anastomosis appears widely patent with no bleeding or bubbles observed.
